sections.title: Initial Orders & Workup
items: EKG — STEMI if STE >1mm in 2 or more contiguous leads
HS-troponin x2: draw now and at +1 hour
Stat TTE if trop High — wall motion abnormalities (WMA)
BMP, CBC, coagulation studies, BNP, lipids
CXR (widened mediastinum = dissection; pneumothorax; pulmonary edema)
If STEMI: activate cath lab
NSTEMI/Unstable Angina: calculate GRACE score
GRACE low risk: serial troponins — if Nl, outpt stress test; if elevated, PCI
GRACE high risk: call cardiology

drugs.name: Aspirin
drugs.notes: Non-enteric coated. Give immediately. Consult cardiology before loading P2Y12 if CABG possible.

drugs.name: Nitroglycerin
drugs.notes: IV drip: start 10 mcg/min, titrate by 10 mcg/min q10min. STOP if SBP <100.

drugs.name: Heparin (UFH)
drugs.notes: Consult cardiology before initiating. Weight-based dosing.

drugs.name: Clopidogrel
drugs.notes: HOLD 5 days before CABG. Do not load if CABG likely.

drugs.name: Ticagrelor
drugs.notes: HOLD 5 days before CABG. Preferred over clopidogrel in most ACS.

drugs.name: Metoprolol succinate
drugs.notes: Target resting HR 50-60. Start low, titrate up.

drugs.name: Lisinopril
drugs.notes: Use if EF <40%.

drugs.name: Atorvastatin
drugs.notes: High-intensity statin. Start in all ACS regardless of baseline LDL.

sections.title: Acute Management
items:
Circulation: assess pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: assess patency and protect.
Breathing: assess WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
!!Assess hemodynamic stability!! — BP, mentation, perfusion, chest pain, SOB
If!!unstable + tachycardia!! → synchronized cardioversion (sedate if possible)
If!!unstable + bradycardia!! → atropine 0.5-1 mg IV + transcutaneous pacing
Get!!12-lead EKG immediately!! — do NOT treat based on monitor strip alone
IV access, telemetry, pulse oximetry

sections.title: Chart Check / History
items: Onset — sudden vs gradual? Duration? Intermittent vs sustained?
Character — fast, slow, regular, irregular, "skipping" (ectopy), "fluttering" (SVT/AFib)
Associated sx — chest pain, SOB, lightheadedness, syncope/presyncope, diaphoresis
Triggers — exertion, caffeine, alcohol, stress, position change, Valsalva terminates it?
Prior episodes — prior arrhythmia dx? Cardioversion hx? Ablation?
Cardiac hx — structural heart disease, CHF, valve disease, CAD, cardiomyopathy
Medications — digoxin, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, antiarrhythmics, QT-prolonging drugs
Stimulants — caffeine, cocaine, amphetamines, energy drinks, pseudoephedrine, theophylline
Thyroid disease — hyper/hypothyroid, recent thyroid medication changes
Recent illness — fever, dehydration, anemia, PE risk factors
Family hx — sudden cardiac death, WPW, long QT, Brugada, HCM

pairs.value: HR (rate, regularity), BP (hypotension?), RR, SpO2, temp (fever → ↑ HR)
Diaphoretic, anxious, ill-appearing? Hemodynamically stable?
Regular vs irregular rhythm, murmurs (valvular disease, HCM), S3 (CHF), JVD (R heart failure, PE)
Crackles (pulmonary edema), wheezing, clear (reassuring)
Thyromegaly, JVD, cannon A waves (AV dissociation → complete heart block or VT)
Peripheral edema, cool/mottled (poor perfusion), asymmetric leg swelling (DVT → PE)
AMS (hypoperfusion), tremor (thyrotoxicosis), focal deficits (thromboembolism from AFib)
Subjective awareness of heartbeat — may be fast, slow, irregular, or "pounding"; NOT always arrhythmia
QRS <120 ms — supraventricular origin (sinus tach, SVT, AFib, aflutter, MAT)
QRS ≥120 ms — assume!!VT until proven otherwise!! (also: SVT w/ aberrancy, pre-excitation, paced rhythm)
Supraventricular tachycardia — regular, narrow complex, 150-250 bpm; AVNRT is most common type
Wolff-Parkinson-White — accessory pathway; delta waves + short PR on baseline EKG;!!avoid AV nodal blockers in AFib + WPW!!
>500 ms = ↑ risk of torsades de pointes; check drug list, electrolytes
AFib → rate control vs cardioversion
Atrial flutter 2:1 → rate control
!!VT until proven otherwise!! → amiodarone or cardioversion
SVT (AVNRT/AVRT) → vagal maneuvers, adenosine
MAT → treat underlying cause (COPD, hypoxia, hypomagnesemia)
Complete heart block → pacing
WPW —!!avoid AV nodal blockers!! (digoxin, CCB, adenosine) if in AFib

pairs.key: Sinus tach (P before every QRS, regular, 100-150)
pairs.value: NOT primary arrhythmia — find the underlying cause (pain, fever, hypovolemia, PE, sepsis, anxiety)
Usually benign; treat underlying cause; if symptomatic or PVC burden >10% → cardiology f/u
!!Torsades de pointes!! → Mg 2 g IV, overdrive pacing; stop QT-prolonging drugs
AFib/flutter, SVT, VT, WPW, sick sinus syndrome, heart block, PACs, PVCs
Valvular disease (mitral), HCM, cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, pericarditis
!!Hypokalemia!!, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, hyperthyroidism, hypoglycemia
Stimulants (caffeine, cocaine, amphetamines), theophylline, pseudoephedrine, digoxin toxicity, QT-prolonging drugs
Dehydration, anemia, hemorrhage, PE, sepsis — sinus tach as compensatory response
Hyperthyroidism / thyroid storm, pheochromocytoma
Panic attack, anxiety — dx of exclusion after cardiac causes r/o
Syncope w/ palpitations, exertional palpitations, family hx sudden cardiac death, structural heart disease, wide complex tachycardia

drugs.name: Adenosine
drugs.dose: 6 mg rapid IV push → 12 mg if no response → 12 mg; use proximal IV + rapid saline flush
drugs.contraindications: !!AFib w/ WPW!! (can cause VF), 2nd/3rd degree heart block, asthma (relative)
drugs.notes: Diagnostic & therapeutic for SVT (AVNRT/AVRT). Warn pt: transient chest tightness, flushing. Half-life ~10 sec

drugs.name: Metoprolol
drugs.dose: 5 mg IV over 2 min; may repeat q5 min × 3 doses; then 25-50 mg PO q6h
drugs.contraindications: Acute decompensated CHF, 2nd/3rd degree block, severe bradycardia, cocaine use, WPW
drugs.notes: Rate control for AFib/flutter, SVT refractory to adenosine, sinus tach from thyrotoxicosis

drugs.name: Diltiazem
drugs.dose: 0.25 mg/kg IV over 2 min (typically 15-20 mg); may repeat 0.35 mg/kg; then infusion 5-15 mg/hr
drugs.notes: Rate control for AFib/flutter. Effective if BB contraindicated or insufficient

drugs.name: Amiodarone
drugs.dose: 150 mg IV over 10 min → 1 mg/min × 6 hrs → 0.5 mg/min × 18 hrs
drugs.contraindications: Severe sinus node disease, 2nd/3rd degree block w/o pacer, cardiogenic shock
drugs.notes: For!!stable wide complex tachycardia (VT)!! or rate/rhythm control in AFib. Safe in structural heart disease

drugs.name: Magnesium sulfate
drugs.notes: !!First-line for torsades de pointes!!; also replete if Mg <2.0 (↑ arrhythmia risk)

drugs.name: Atropine
drugs.dose: 0.5-1 mg IV q3-5 min; max 3 mg
drugs.notes: For symptomatic bradycardia. Temporizing — prepare for pacing if no response

sections.title: Ongoing Management & Pearls
items: !!Sinus tachycardia is NOT primary arrhythmia!! — always find the underlying cause (sepsis, PE, hypovolemia, pain, anxiety)
!!Wide complex tachycardia = VT until proven otherwise!! — treating VT as SVT can be fatal
!!AFib + WPW: avoid AV nodal blockers!! (adenosine, diltiazem, digoxin, metoprolol) → can cause VF; use procainamide or cardioversion
Check K+ & Mg2+ early — both hypokalemia & hypomagnesemia are easily correctable arrhythmia causes
Regular rate exactly ~150 bpm → think aflutter 2:1 (may need to unmask w/ adenosine to see flutter waves)
Panic attacks / anxiety are dx of exclusion — always r/o cardiac causes first
If palpitations + syncope or family hx of sudden death → high-risk; requires inpt workup + cardiology consult
Digoxin toxicity can cause almost ANY arrhythmia — always check level if pt is on digoxin
Caffeine, energy drinks, cocaine, amphetamines are common precipitants — ask specifically

sections.title: Orders
items: !!12-lead EKG!! — cornerstone of evaluation; compare to prior EKG if avail
BMP — K+, Mg2+ (correct if low; both ↑ arrhythmia risk)
CBC — anemia can cause sinus tachycardia
TSH — hyperthyroidism is treatable & common cause
Troponin — if chest pain or concern for ischemia-driven arrhythmia
Digoxin level — if on digoxin (toxicity causes multiple arrhythmia types)
BNP/NT-proBNP — if concern for heart failure
D-dimer / CTA — if PE suspected (sinus tach + pleuritic pain + leg swelling)
Review medication list for QT-prolonging drugs (www.crediblemeds.org)
TTE — if new arrhythmia, suspected structural heart disease, or unexplained
telemetry monitoring
Holter or event monitor if intermittent & no acute arrhythmia captured on EKG

sections.title: RECAP Template
items: **R**ecognize — palpitations w/ hemodynamic assessment; EKG rhythm identification
**E**valuate — 12-lead EKG, K+/Mg2+, TSH, troponin; stable vs unstable?
**C**ritical actions — cardiovert if unstable; adenosine for SVT; amiodarone for stable VT; correct electrolytes
**A**ssess response — rhythm converted? Rate controlled? Hemodynamics stable? Symptoms resolved?
**P**lan — telemetry monitoring, cardiology consult if new arrhythmia or structural disease, outpt Holter if intermittent

pairs.value: Hemodynamic instability, VT, wide complex tachycardia, cardioversion required, new heart block, torsades
New AFib, controlled SVT, recurrent symptomatic arrhythmia, new structural findings on TTE
Benign PACs/PVCs, sinus tach w/ identified & treated cause, known AFib w/ adequate rate control, normal workup
New arrhythmia, syncope w/ palpitations, structural heart disease, WPW, wide complex tachycardia, family hx sudden death
Holter/event monitor for intermittent sx, outpt TTE if not done inpt, medication titration

topics.title: Tachycardia
topics.subtitle: ACLS approach — narrow vs wide, regular vs irregular

sections.title: Acute Management
items:
Circulation: assess pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: assess patency and protect.
Breathing: assess WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
!!12-lead EKG immediately!! — monitor strip alone is insufficient for dx
Assess hemodynamic stability: BP, mentation, signs of shock, chest pain, pulmonary edema
!!If UNSTABLE (hypotension, AMS, shock)!! → immediate!!synchronized cardioversion!!
If STABLE → classify rhythm: narrow vs wide, regular vs irregular, then treat per algorithm below
**Narrow regular (SVT)**: Vagal maneuvers → adenosine 6mg rapid IVP → repeat 12mg → diltiazem or metoprolol → cardioversion 50—100J
**Narrow regular (AFL)**: Rate control (diltiazem, metoprolol) → cardioversion 50J if unstable
**Narrow irregular (AFib)**: Rate control (diltiazem, metoprolol, amiodarone if CHF) → cardioversion 120-200J if unstable
**Narrow irregular (MAT)**: Treat underlying cause (hypoK, hypoMg, hypoxia). Diltiazem if symptomatic.!!Avoid beta-blockers if lung disease!!
**Wide regular (VT)**:!!Assume VT!! → procainamide 20-50 mg/min IV (max 17 mg/kg) → amiodarone 150mg IV if CHF/AMI → cardioversion 100J
**Wide irregular (polyVT/Torsades)**:!!Magnesium 2g IV!! → emergent defibrillation (unsynchronized) 200J → stop QT-prolonging drugs
**Wide irregular (AFib + WPW)**:!!NO AV nodal blockers (diltiazem, metoprolol, adenosine, digoxin)!! → procainamide or cardioversion

sections.title: Chart Check / History
items: !!Is this sinus tachycardia?!! If yes — treat underlying cause (pain, fever, hypovolemia, anemia, PE, hypoxia, anxiety)
Known cardiac history — prior arrhythmias, WPW, CHF, MI, structural heart disease
Medications: beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin, antiarrhythmics (current and adherence)
QT-prolonging drugs: antipsychotics (haloperidol, quetiapine), antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, macrolides), ondansetron, methadone
Electrolyte history — last K, Mg values; diuretic use
Substance use — cocaine, amphetamines, caffeine, energy drinks, alcohol withdrawal
Thyroid history — hyperthyroidism, thyroid storm
Surgical/procedural history — recent cardiac surgery, ablation, pacemaker/ICD

sections.title: Exam (if able)
items: Hemodynamic status: BP, MAP, cap. refill, peripheral perfusion
Mentation: alertness, orientation —!!AMS = unstable!!
Cardiac: regular vs irregular rhythm, murmurs, S3/S4 gallop
Lungs: crackles (pulmonary edema → unstable), wheezing
JVP: elevated (CHF, tamponade, PE) vs flat (hypovolemia)
Extremities: cool/mottled (shock), edema (CHF)
Skin: diaphoresis (sympathetic activation), flushing
Thyroid: goiter, tremor, lid lag (thyrotoxicosis)

pairs.value: Heart rate >100 bpm. Clinically significant usually >130-150 bpm
**1)** Is this sinus tachycardia? **2)** Is the QRS narrow (<120ms) or wide (≥120ms)? **3)** Is the rhythm regular or irregular?
SVT, AFib, AFL, MAT, sinus tach
!!Assume ventricular tachycardia until proven otherwise!! May also be SVT w/ aberrancy or pre-excitation
!!Hypotension, AMS, shock, ischemic chest pain, acute heart failure!! → immediate synchronized cardioversion
Is tachycardia THE problem, or SIGN of another problem? Sinus tach in sick pt = compensatory — treat the cause, not the rate
Pain, fever, hypovolemia, anemia, PE, hypoxia, hyperthyroidism, anxiety, medications — treat the cause, not the rate
Narrow, regular, abrupt onset/offset, rate 150-250. Vagal maneuvers → adenosine
AFib: irregularly irregular. AFL: regular ~150 (2:1 block). Rate control or cardioversion
Irregular, ≥3 P-wave morphologies. Associated w/ COPD, hypoxia, hypoK/Mg
Wide, regular, rate 150-250.!!Assume VT if wide-complex regular!!. Procainamide or amiodarone
Wide, irregular, QRS axis shifts.!!Magnesium 2g IV!! + defibrillation. Stop QT-prolonging drugs
Wide, irregular, variable morphology.!!NO AV nodal blockers — causes VFib!!. Procainamide or cardioversion
Wide-complex but supraventricular. Difficult to distinguish from VT —!!treat as VT if uncertain!!

drugs.name: Adenosine
drugs.dose: 6mg rapid IVP→12mg if no response (may repeat 12mg x1)
drugs.route: IV (rapid push via large bore proximal IV + 20mL NS flush)
drugs.contraindications: Wide complex irregular rhythm, WPW w/AFib, lung transplant (reduce dose by 50%)
drugs.notes: For!!regular narrow-complex SVT!!. Warn pt: transient chest tightness, flushing, sense of doom. Half-life ~6 sec. Record rhythm strip during push.

drugs.name: Diltiazem
drugs.dose: 15-20mg (0.25 mg/kg) IV over 2 min, then 5-15 mg/h infusion
drugs.contraindications: !!WPW, wide complex tachycardia!!, hypotension, severe CHF, sick sinus, 2nd/3rd degree block
drugs.notes: Rate control for AFib/FLutter, SVT refractory to adenosine, MAT. Repeat bolus 20-25mg after 15 min if needed.

drugs.name: Metoprolol
drugs.dose: 5mg IV q5min x 3 doses, then 25-50mg PO
drugs.contraindications: !!WPW!!, cardiogenic shock, severe bradycardia, cocaine use(arguable), severe asthma/COPD
drugs.notes: Rate control for AFib, AFL, SVT. Avoid in acute HF — use amiodarone instead.

drugs.name: Procainamide
drugs.dose: 20-50 mg/min IV until arrhythmia suppressed (max 17 mg/kg or 1g); maintenance 1-4 mg/min x 6h
drugs.notes: !!First-line for stable wide complex VT.!! PROCAMIO trial: 67% termination vs 38% for amiodarone.!!Stop if QRS widens >50% or hypotension develops.!!

drugs.name: Amiodarone
drugs.dose: 150mg IV over 10 min, then 1 mg/min x 6h (360mg), then 0.5 mg/min x 18h (540mg)
drugs.notes: Second-line for VT.!!Preferred if AMI or LV dysfunction!!. Also for rate control in AFib w/ CHF. Monitor for hypotension during bolus.

drugs.name: Magnesium sulfate
drugs.notes: !!First-line for Torsades de Pointes / polymorphic VT w/ prolonged QT.!! Also correct hypoMg in any tachyarrhythmia.

drugs.name: Digoxin
drugs.notes: Rate control for AFib/AFL!!in CHF pts!!. Slow onset (hours). Narrow therapeutic index — check level.

sections.title: Ongoing Management
items: Recheck 12-lead EKG after each intervention — confirm rhythm conversion or rate control
Replete electrolytes aggressively: K >4.0, Mg >2.0 for any arrhythmia
SVT: if recurrent after adenosine, start maintenance rate control (diltiazem drip or metoprolol)
AFib/AFL: target HR <110 bpm at rest. If new-onset <48h, consider rhythm control (cardioversion ± anticoagulation)
VT: after cardioversion or chemical conversion, start maintenance antiarrhythmic (amiodarone drip). Cardiology consult for ICD eval
Torsades: correct underlying cause (stop offending drug, replete K/Mg). Consider isoproterenol or overdrive pacing if recurrent
Address underlying etiology: sepsis, PE, thyroid storm, hemorrhage, pain, substance use
Monitor for pro-arrhythmic effects of antiarrhythmics (QT prolongation, bradycardia, hypotension)

pairs.value: Essential — classify narrow vs wide, regular vs irregular. Compare to prior EKG if avail
K and Mg are critical — hypoK and hypoMg are reversible causes of tachyarrhythmias
Often not on standard BMP — must order separately. Low Mg → Torsades risk
If ischemia suspected or sustained tachycardia (demand ischemia)
Screen for hyperthyroidism if new-onset tachyarrhythmia w/o clear cause
Anemia (compensatory sinus tach), leukocytosis (infection/sepsis)
If concern for shock or poor perfusion
All tachyarrhythmias — monitor for deterioration or response to tx
!!Required for any wide-complex tachycardia!! may convert to VFib
!!Any VT, hemodynamically unstable arrhythmia, Torsades, cardioversion required, vasopressor-dependent!!
New-onset AFib/AFL, SVT requiring IV rate control, wide-complex tachycardia (resolved), post-cardioversion monitoring
Sinus tachycardia w/ identified treatable cause (pain, fever, dehydration) and rate improving
SVT: 50-100J | AFL: 50J | AFib: 120-200J | Stable VT: 100J | Unstable wide irregular/Torsades:!!200J unsynchronized (defibrillation)!!
!!NEVER give AV nodal blockers (adenosine, diltiazem, metoprolol, digoxin) to AFib w/ WPW — causes VFib and death!!

sections.title: ABCs — Stable or Unstable?
items:
Circulation: assess pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: assess patency and protect.
Breathing: assess WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
Unstable = SBP <90, AMS, chest pain, SOB → give atropine and get pads ready
Hypoxia or ↑ WOB → give O2 first (hypoxia itself causes bradycardia)
telemetry, EKG, IV access
If unstable: atropine 1mg IV → transcutaneous pacing → epi drip

sections.title: Chart Check & Exam
items: Prior EKGs and TTE — baseline rhythm, conduction disease, prior pacemaker
Meds in past 24-48h: beta blockers, calcium channel blockers, amiodarone, digoxin, opioids, SSRIs
Hx.: hypothyroidism, heart disease, prior cardiac surgery, OSA, syncope
Exam: mentation, BP, cap refill, JVP, signs of volume overload
Can't miss: inferior ACS (check II, III, aVF for STE), complete heart block, BRASH syndrome (BB/CCB toxicity + ↑K)

pairs.value: Normal P before each QRS, rate <60. Physiologic (athletes, sleep), vagal, hypothyroid, drugs.
PR >200ms, every P conducts. Usually benign.
Progressive PR lengthening until P drops. Usually benign, vagal.
Fixed PR w/ sudden non-conducted P waves. High risk of progression to CHB — needs pacer.
P waves and QRS completely dissociated. Requires pacing.
STE in II, III, aVF w/ bradycardia — RCA occlusion affecting AV node. Get cath lab involved.
Inferior MI, complete HB, BRASH syndrome (BB/CCB toxicity + hyperK), digoxin toxicity
Bradycardia + renal failure + AV nodal blocker + shock + ↑K. Suspect in pts on BB/CCB with AKI, rising K, and disproportionate bradycardia; treat both ↑K and AV nodal blocker toxicity.
SSS, AV nodal disease, ischemia, ↑ vagal tone (pain, OSA, carotid hypersensitivity)
Myocarditis, amyloid, sarcoid, malignancy, autoimmune (lupus, RA)
Lyme disease (classic — complete HB in young pt), sepsis, endocarditis
Beta-blockers, CCBs, amiodarone, digoxin, opioids, SSRIs, lithium
Hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, ↑K, hypocalcemia, hypothermia
Cushing's reflex (↑ ICP → severe HTN + bradycardia), elevated ICP
Autonomic dysfunction from Guillain-Barré can cause marked bradycardia, pauses, or labile hemodynamics
Athletes, sleep — asymptomatic and benign
Treat when bradycardia is causing hypotension, altered mentation, ischemia, shock, or acute heart failure.
Use atropine early, but do not delay pacing or vasoactive support if pt is unstable or high-grade AV block is suspected.
Move promptly to transcutaneous pacing, epinephrine or dopamine infusion, and transvenous pacing when atropine fails or is unlikely to work.
Identify inferior MI, medication toxicity, electrolyte derangement, hypothermia, or intrinsic conduction disease and treat the driver.

sections.title: Initial Orders & Workup
items: 12-lead EKG— rhythm, inferior MI, PR interval, QRS width
iStat: VBG, lactate
Labs: BMP (K, Ca, Mg), CMP (liver and renal function), TSH, troponin
Stat TTE: WMA, effusion, tamponade, LV function
MAR: BB, CCBs, digoxin levels

sections.title: Treatment — Unstable
items: 1. Atropine 1mg IV q3-5min→ 3mg total —first-line
2. If atropine fails (or no IV): transcutaneous pacing: give fentanyl 25mcg + midazolam 2mg
Start pacing at 60-80 bpm, ⬆mA until electrical and mechanical capture
Epinephrine drip if severe hypotension: 2-10 mcg/min
If no CHF/overload: trial 500mL-1L NS pressure-bagged
Transvenous pacing if CHB/refractory— call EP/cardiology

drugs.name: Atropine
drugs.notes: First-line for unstable bradycardia. Less effective for high-grade AV block — do not delay pacing.

drugs.name: Glucagon
drugs.dose: 10mg IV bolus, repeat 1x q10 min; if response, infusion at 3-5 mg/hr
drugs.notes: Beta-blocker OR CCB OD. Bypasses blocked receptors. Repeat bolus if no response in 5-10 min.

drugs.name: Calcium gluconate
drugs.notes: CCB OD — competes at calcium channel. Also for ↑K (BRASH).

drugs.name: Regular insulin (HIE)
drugs.dose: 1 unit/kg IV bolus, then 0.5 units/kg/hr gtt
drugs.notes: CCB/BB OD — insulin euglycemic therapy. Give w/ D50. POC glu q30min.

drugs.name: Digoxin immune Fab
drugs.notes: Digoxin tox. Give over 30 min. 1 vial = 40mg Fab. Empiric dose: 10 vials for acute OD, 3-6 vials for chr toxicity.

drugs.name: Naloxone
drugs.notes: Opioid-induced bradycardia. Consider drip if large ingestion: 2/3 of reversal dose per hour.

drugs.name: Atropine (for organophosphate)
drugs.dose: 2-5mg IV, double dose q5min until secretions dry
drugs.notes: Organophosphate/nerve agent toxicity. THEN add pralidoxime 1-2g IV over 15-30 min.

sections.title: Indications for Permanent Pacemaker (PPM)
items: Symptomatic sinus bradycardia w/ or w/o pauses
Complete heart block (3rd degree AV block)
Symptomatic Mobitz II (even if asymptomatic — high risk of progression)
Syncope secondary to bradycardia or documented pause >3 seconds
Chronotropic incompetence causing exercise intolerance
Consult electrophysiology (EP) for all complete heart block and symptomatic Mobitz II

sections.title: References & Resources
items: Kusumoto FM, Schoenfeld MH, Barrett C, et al. 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay. Circulation. 2019;140(8):e382e482. https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000628

sections.title: First Impression — Guiding Questions
items:
Circulation: assess pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: assess patency and protect.
Breathing: assess WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
General appearance: Mentation, acute distress,can they protect their airway?
Current oxygenation: what are they on?, improving?, do you need to intubate?
Is this driven by ↓cardiac output or ↓SVR?
Distributive, hypovolemic, cardiogenic, or obstructive?
Preload: Volume status, venous return, obst to return (tension PTX, tamponade)?
Afterload: SVR, arterial stiffness, obst to outflow (valves, HOCM)?
Fluid responsive? (hypovolemia, early sepsis)

sections.title: ABCs
items: Non-responsive (GCS <8): check pulse → intubate
Respiratory distress or hypoxia: NRB 15L → HFNC → BiPAP (COPD) or CPAP (CHF) → intubate
PaO2 <60 despite NRB → start HFNC; PaCO2 >45 → BiPAP; pH <7.25 → consider early intubation
Red flags (intubate): pooling secretions, hemoptysis, worsening respiratory distress
AFib w/ RVR: if HR >150-170 and HoTN → HR likely cause → rate control > cardioversion
Active GI bleed: MTP protocol (1:1:1 pRBC:FFP:platelets)
Undifferentiated hypotension: 2L LR wide open (pressure bag, if no CHF/AKI), infectious workup, start/broaden Abx, start Levo if needed

sections.title: Chart Check
items: Reason for hospitalization and relevant comorbidities
Baseline BP range & mentation
Check last several BPs and timing on monitor — can you trust the readings? Is the cuff in the right place?
Acuity of change — sudden vs gradual
Day events: recent meds, procedures, blood transfusions?
Current IV access — do we need more?
Code status and decision-makers
MAR: opioids, AC (bleeding risk), BB, CCBs, TCAs

pairs.value: Fever or hypothermia, BP trend, HR, SpO2, RR — note trajectory not single values
Cap refill, warm vs cold extremities, skin mottling, diaphoresis
Tamponade (pericardial effusion + RV collapse), RV failure (BIG RV, septal bowing), LV function (EF)
B-lines (pulmonary edema), absent lung sliding (PTX)
Flat/collapsible IVC = hypovolemic/distributive; plethoric IVC = cardiogenic/obstructive
Flat = hypovolemic/distributive; elevated = cardiogenic, tamponade, tension PTX, RV failure
Sepsis (most common), anaphylaxis, neurogenic shock, adrenal crisis, liver failure, thyroid storm. Warm extremities, low SVR, high CO.
Hemorrhage (GI, chest, abdomen, retroperitoneum, thigh fracture), diuresis, poor PO intake, vomiting, diarrhea, third-spacing
Arrhythmia (AFib RVR, VT/VF), ischemia/MI, decompensated CHF, valvulopathy (acute MR/AR), myocarditis
Tension PTX (absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, JVD), PE (massive), cardiac tamponade (Beck's triad: JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds)
Opioids, sedatives, BB/CCB OD, TCA OD (wide QRS, QTc prolongation)
Adrenal crisis (↑K, ↓Na, hypoglycemia), hypothyroidism, hypocalcemia
Severe dysautonomia can produce hypotension, bradycardia, and hemodynamic instability in Guillain-Barré syndrome
Recognize shock early, secure airway and oxygenation as needed, establish IV access, start monitoring, and assess perfusion repeatedly.
Use fluids when hypovolemia or distributive shock is likely, but avoid uncritical boluses in cardiogenic or obstructive shock.
Start vasopressors early when MAP remains low after initial resuscitation; add inotropes when pump failure predominates.
Shock resolves only when the underlying process is treated: bleeding control, sepsis therapy, reperfusion, decompression, thrombolysis, or endocrine rescue as appropriate.

sections.title: Workup
items: Infectious: CBC w/ diff, CXR, UA/UCx, blood cultures x2, lactate
Metabolic: CMP (calcium, renal/liver), TSH and cortisol if c/f AI
Cardiac: EKG, troponin, NT-proBNP
Respiratory: ABG/VBG
Toxin: MAR for opioids, AC (bleed), BB/CCB, TCAs (give bicarb if QRS >100ms)
CT-PE if c/f PE
Consider calcium gluconate if low iCal (esp after blood transfusions)

pairs.value: Abx + source control, 30 mL/kg LR bolus (unless CHF/renal/cirrhosis), Levo → vasopressin → epinephrine. Hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h + fludrocortisone 50 mg daily if on 2 pressors or AI suspected.
Epinephrine 0.3mg IM (thigh) STAT. IV fluids, diphenhydramine 50mg IV, methylprednisolone 125mg IV, H2 blocker. Epinephrine gtt if refractory.
MTP 1:1:1 (pRBC:FFP:platelets), calcium gluconate after every 4 units pRBC. Source control (endoscopy, IR, surgery).
Levo first, then inotrope (dobutamine or milrinone). BiPAP for pulmonary edema. Avoid intubation if possible. Treat cause (PCI for ACS).
Immediate needle decompression: 2nd ICS midclavicular line, then chest tube. Do not wait for CXR.
Pericardiocentesis — emergent if hemodynamically unstable. Avoid positive pressure ventilation (reduces RV preload). Volume to maintain preload while arranging drainage.
Surgical or catheter embolectomy if hemodynamically unstable. Thrombolytics (alteplase 100mg over 2h) if Heparin drip.
Sodium bicarb 1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus if QRS >100ms or HoTN→GTT to pH 7.45-7.55. Avoid physostigmine.
Hydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT, aggressive IV fluids, correct hypoglycemia. Do not delay for labs.

drugs.name: Norepinephrine (Levophed)
drugs.notes: First-line vasopressor for all shock types except anaphylaxis. Alpha + beta activity. START PERIPHERALLY→ Use central line if uptrending + need vaso.

drugs.name: Vasopressin
drugs.notes: Add-on to NE in septic shock. Do not titrate.

drugs.name: Epinephrine
drugs.notes: Anaphylaxis first-line (IM). Third-line in septic shock. Strong alpha + beta. Can cause lactic acidosis at high doses.

drugs.name: Phenylephrine
drugs.notes: Pure alpha agonist. Use if tachyarrhythmia on norepinephrine. Reduces CO —

drugs.name: Dobutamine
drugs.notes: Inotrope for cardiogenic shock. Start AFTER vasopressor. May drop BP — start low.

drugs.name: Hydrocortisone
drugs.notes: Refractory septic shock on 2+ vasopressors, or adrenal crisis.

drugs.name: Sodium bicarbonate
drugs.notes: TCA OD (QRS >100ms), severe acidosis (pH <7.1), hyperK. Target pH 7.45-7.55 for TCA.

drugs.name: Calcium gluconate
drugs.notes: Give after every 4 units pRBC during massive transfusion (citrate chelates calcium). Also for CCB OD and ↑K.

sections.title: Pearls & Pitfalls
items: Confirm BP cuff placement
Flat IVC on POCUS does not always mean hypovolemia in ventilated pt
In undifferentiated shock: treat empirically→workup. Low threshold for pressors
NEVER give inotrope BEFORE vasopressor for hypotensive cardiogenic shock(drop BP further)
Positive pressure ventilation reduces preload — use w/ caution in tamponade, RV failure
Lactic acidosis in TCA OD is from seizures and hypoperfusion — bicarb treats the cardiotoxicity, not the lactate
Adrenal crisis mimics septic shock perfectly: Consider in pts on chronic steroids or w/ known adrenal disease

sections.title: GI Hemorrhage — Special Considerations
items: Emergent upper endoscopy is first-line for most upper GI bleeds
Exception: aortic surgery within past 5 years → CT angiogram first to r/o aortoenteric fistula before scope
Exception: exsanguinating duodenal ulcer w/ active extravasation on CTA → consider IR directly (posterior duodenal ulcers difficult to control endoscopically)
MTP blood bank order: 10 units pRBC, 6 units FFP, 1 unit platelets (uncrossed)
Massive transfusion ratio: pRBC:FFP:platelets = 1:1:1 (surgical data supports 4:1:1)
Calcium gluconate after every 4 units pRBC — citrate in pRBCs chelates ionized calcium
TXA 1g IV if within 3 hours of hemorrhage onset

topics.title: HTN
topics.subtitle: Asymptomatic severe HTN vs hypertensive emergency

sections.title: Acute Management
items:
Circulation: assess pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Airway: assess patency and protect.
Breathing: assess WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation.
Repeat BP manually and use an appropriate cuff size before escalating treatment for a single extreme reading.
If chest pain, dyspnea, neuro deficits, altered mentation, flash pulmonary edema, AKI, or aortic pain are present, treat as possible hypertensive emergency.
If pt looks well and has no acute end-organ symptoms, avoid reflexive IV antihypertensives and rapid BP reduction.

sections.title: Chart Check / History
items: Baseline BP pattern and whether this is chronic uncontrolled HTN or a new abrupt rise
Missed home antihypertensives, clonidine withdrawal, pain, anxiety, urinary retention, alcohol withdrawal, stimulant use, steroid use
Kidney disease, heart failure, stroke history, pregnancy, endocrine disease, OSA
Recent meds: NSAIDs, decongestants, stimulants, cocaine, amphetamines, MAOIs, calcineurin inhibitors
Ask specifically about headache, vision change, chest pain, dyspnea, focal deficits, confusion, and decreased urine output

pairs.value: Is pt comfortable and mentating normally, or ill-appearing with respiratory distress, confusion, or pain?
Headache alone is nonspecific; focal deficits, encephalopathy, seizure, or visual loss suggest emergency
Chest pain, pulmonary edema, increased WOB, crackles, new murmur, or poor perfusion raise concern for end-organ injury
Look for edema, JVD, oliguria, or signs of AKI or heart failure
Marked BP elevation without acute target-organ injury. This is usually managed with confirmation, symptom review, medication adjustment, and close follow-up rather than IV rescue therapy.
Severe BP elevation with acute target-organ injury such as encephalopathy, stroke, ACS, pulmonary edema, aortic syndrome, or AKI.
Older term for severe asymptomatic HTN. In practice, focus on whether acute organ injury is present rather than the label.
Pain, anxiety, missed medications, volume overload, withdrawal states, stimulants, sleep apnea, urinary retention
AKI/CKD, hyperaldosteronism, pheochromocytoma, Cushing syndrome, thyroid disease, pregnancy-related HTN
Aortic dissection, ACS, acute pulmonary edema, intracranial hemorrhage, PRES, ischemic stroke, eclampsia
If symptoms or signs suggest acute target-organ injury, switch from the asymptomatic HTN pathway to hypertensive emergency evaluation and treatment.

sections.title: Management
items: asymptomatic severe HTN or true hypertensive emergency.

pairs.value: Confirm reading, treat pain or agitation, restart missed home medications when appropriate, avoid IV pushes.
Do not rapidly normalize BP in asymptomatic pts; abrupt reduction can worsen cerebral, coronary, or renal perfusion.
If BP remains markedly elevated after repeat checks and reversible triggers are addressed, use or intensify oral antihypertensives based on the pt's home regimen and comorbidities.
Escalate immediately if new neuro findings, chest pain, pulmonary edema, AKI, pregnancy-related severe features, or aortic syndrome concerns emerge.
Manual BP with correct cuff size, both arms if dissection is a concern, and trend over time rather than one isolated value
BMP/CMP, CBC, UA, ECG; add troponin, BNP, CXR, head CT, or pregnancy testing only if symptoms or exam suggest organ injury
Check MAR and home med reconciliation for missed antihypertensives, interacting drugs, or withdrawal syndromes
Frequent BP reassessment after pain control, bladder decompression, restarting meds, or oral regimen changes
Asymptomatic pts without evidence of acute organ injury can usually stay on the floor with oral regimen adjustment and close follow-up planning.
Hypertensive emergency, rapidly evolving symptoms, IV antihypertensive need, or major end-organ injury requires monitored or ICU-level care.

sections.title: Acute Management
items: Airway: assess patency and protect. Breathing: assess WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: assess pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
!!Airway!! — stridor, muffled voice, drooling, inability to speak, or rapidly worsening fatigue = call airway team early
!!Breathing!! — SpO2, RR, WOB, accessory muscle use, mentation, ability to speak full sentences
Sit upright; start oxygen immediately if hypoxemic or in marked distress
Target SpO2 94-98% for most pts; 88-92% if at risk for hypercapnic respiratory failure (COPD, obesity hypoventilation, neuromuscular weakness) while awaiting gas
If severe hypoxemia despite standard oxygen → escalate to HFNC
If acute hypercapnic respiratory failure or cardiogenic pulmonary edema and pt can protect airway → start NIV/BiPAP early
If impending arrest, inprotect airway, severe fatigue, or worsening hypoxemia/hypercapnia despite noninvasive support → intubate
Parallel question: airway problem, alveolar problem, obstructive problem, vascular problem, or pump failure?

items: Confirm it is real: check waveform, probe, perfusion, and interface. Get ABG if severe or unclear.
Escalate O2: NC/simple mask → NRB → HFNC. Use NIV early for hypercapnia, COPD, or cardiogenic pulm edema if airway is protected.
Give etiology-specific treatment: nebs, diuresis/nitrates, abx, anticoagulation, decompression, or urgent airway management.
Reassess often. If worsening fatigue, mentation, CO2, or hypoxemia, intubate before collapse.

sections.title: Acute Management
items: !!Airway!! — if massive hemoptysis (>100-600 mL/24h):!!intubate early!! w/ largest ETT possible; death is from asphyxiation, NOT exsanguination

0 mm to 8.5 mm is generally required to allow for adequate ventilation around the bronchoscope. While smaller tubes (e.g., 6.0-7.0 mm) can accommodate scope, they risk high airway pressures and auto-PEEP
!!Position pt w/ BLEEDING side down!! — protects contralateral lung from aspiration of blood
!!Breathing!! — supplemental O2, continuous SpO2; suction airway as needed
!!Circulation!! — 2 large-bore IVs, type & screen; if massive → activate MTP
Call interventional pulmonology AND IR simultaneously for massive hemoptysis
If intubating: consider selective intubation of unaffected bronchus or use bronchial blocker
!!Reverse anticoagulation!! immediately if on blood thinners
CXR and CT chest w/ contrast to identify source

Treat the cause: give Abx for infection, treat cardiac causes, weigh anticoagulation carefully in PE, and start syndrome-specific treatment for DAH.

sections.title: Orders
items: POC glucose immediately
iStat: VBG, Hb, Na, Ca, K
Labs: CBC w/ diff, BMP, LFTs, lactate, TSH, cortisol, ammonia
UA & urine culture, bladder scan
BCxs x2, CXR
Toxicology screen & drug levels (lithium, ASA, AEDs)

drugs.name: Dextrose (D50)
drugs.notes: Hypoglycemia or high suspicion with delayed glucose result.

drugs.name: Thiamine
drugs.notes: Give early if malnutrition, alcoholism, or c/f Wernicke encephalopathy; ideally before glucose if feasible.

drugs.name: Naloxone
drugs.dose: 0.04-0.4 mg IV, titrate; repeat q2-3 min PRN
drugs.notes: Opioid toxicity with hypoventilation; titrate to ventilation, not full arousal.

drugs.name: Flumazenil
drugs.dose: 0.2 mg IV over 15 sec, repeat carefully
drugs.contraindications: Chronic benzo use, seizure disorder, mixed overdose
drugs.notes: Rarely used; can precipitate refractory seizures.

drugs.name: Hypertonic saline 3%
drugs.dose: 100 mL IV bolus, may repeat up to 3 times
drugs.notes: Symptomatic hyponatremic encephalopathy with seizure, severe confusion, or coma.

drugs.name: Lactulose
drugs.dose: 20-30 g PO/NG or 30-45 mL; repeat to 3-4 BMs/day
drugs.notes: First-line for hepatic encephalopathy.

drugs.name: Rifaximin
drugs.notes: Add-on for hepatic encephalopathy, especially recurrent or persistent symptoms.

drugs.name: Lorazepam (Ativan)
drugs.notes: First-line for active seizure or status epilepticus causing encephalopathy.

drugs.name: Levetiracetam (Keppra)
drugs.notes: Use after benzodiazepine or when nonconvulsive status is suspected.

drugs.name: Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin
drugs.dose: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h + pharmacy-dosed vancomycin
drugs.notes: Empiric bacterial meningitis coverage.

drugs.name: Ampicillin
drugs.notes: Add for Listeria risk: older age, pregnancy, or immunocompromise.

drugs.name: Acyclovir
drugs.notes: Add when HSV encephalitis is possible.

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: Give with or before first antibiotic dose if bacterial meningitis is suspected.

drugs.name: Bumetanide
drugs.notes: If pulmonary edema or volume-overload hypercapnia is contributing to encephalopathy.

drugs.name: Haloperidol
drugs.notes: First-line IV/IM. Highest QTc risk.

drugs.name: Quetiapine (Seroquel)
drugs.notes: Smallest QT prolongation. Preferred in elderly or Parkinson's.

drugs.name: Olanzapine
drugs.notes: Moderate QTc risk. Good IM option.

drugs.name: Aripiprazole
drugs.notes: Use if QTc >500. Check EKG after 2nd dose.

drugs.name: Valproic acid
drugs.notes: If QTc >500 & parenteral option needed.

Headache caused by structural, infectious, vascular, or metabolic disease rather than primary headache disorder
Worst headache of life, maximal at onset, vomiting, meningismus, collapse
Unilateral, pulsating, nausea/photophobia, aura possible. Sumatriptan, ketorolac, prochlorperazine, IV fluids.
B/l, band-like, no nausea/photophobia. NSAIDs, acetaminophen.

Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia
Obese young woman, pulsatile tinnitus, papilledema, worse lying flat. LP for pressure + tx.

sections.title: Management
items: If thunderclap HA:!!STAT non-contrast head CT!! → if negative → LP for xanthochromia (r/o SAH)
If focal deficits: consider stroke alert (CT, CTA)
If fever + nuchal rigidity: empiric meningitis tx BEFORE LP (vanc + ceftriaxone + dexamethasone)

drugs.name: Ketorolac
drugs.contraindications: Renal failure, active bleeding, anticoagulation-related high bleed risk
drugs.notes: Common non-opioid option for migraine or tension-type headache.

drugs.name: Prochlorperazine
drugs.notes: Useful for migraine-associated nausea and headache relief. Consider diphenhydramine for akathisia prevention.

drugs.name: Acetaminophen
drugs.notes: Adjunct or first-line option for non-emergent headache syndromes.

drugs.name: Dexamethasone
drugs.notes: Give with empiric meningitis treatment when bacterial meningitis is suspected.

drugs.name: Ceftriaxone
drugs.notes: Empiric meningitis coverage; combine with other agents based on age and risk factors.

pairs.value: SAH, herniation risk, severe meningitis/encephalitis, uncontrolled BP with neuro injury, significant altered mentation
Persistent secondary-headache concern, incomplete workup, uncontrolled pain with red flags, or need for LP / serial neuro exams

topics.title: Syncope
topics.subtitle: Transient LOC — ABCs, EKG, r/o cardiac & neurologic causes

sections.title: Acute Management
items: !!ABCs!! — assess responsiveness, airway, breathing, circulation
Pulse ox — ensure O2 >92% (or >88% if COPD)
!!Continuous cardiac monitoring!! — arrhythmia may be transient
!!12-lead EKG!! — do not delay
IV access, point-of-care glucose

sections.title: Chart Check / History
items: !!Witnessed details!!: manner of collapse, duration down, rhythmic movements
!!Inciting events!!: prolonged standing, upon standing, stressors, pain, poor PO intake, hot weather
!!Prodrome!!: dizziness, nausea, abdominal pain, warmth/flushing
!!Recovery!!: time to baseline, confusion, nausea/vomiting (prolonged postictal → consider seizure)
Head strike: lucid interval (r/o epidural hematoma)
Associated sx: tongue laceration (!!lateral = seizure!!, tip = syncope), loss of urine/stool, palpitations, chest pain, SOB
!!RED FLAGS!!: non-sinus EKG, dyspnea, anemia, HoTN, h/o HF, palpitations, chest pain, exertional, supine syncope, no prodrome
PMHx: seizure, DM, CAD, CHF, valvular dz, arrhythmia, PE risk factors (malignancy, asymmetric leg swelling), prior echo/EKG, h/o clotting, head trauma, FHx SCD
Meds: opioids, benzos, TCAs, hypnotics, anticholinergics, anticoagulants, antihypertensives, diuretics

sections.title: Exam (if able)
items: !!Orthostatic vitals!!: ↓ SBP ≥20 or ↓ DBP ≥10 or ↑ HR ≥30 upon standing (wait 3 min)
General appearance & volume status assessment
Cardiac: rhythm,!!murmur!! (AS, HOCM), S3, S4
Lungs: crackles (pulm edema, PNA)
Extremities: LE edema, asymmetric swelling (DVT/PE risk)
Neuro: focal deficits (stroke/TIA), postictal state, tongue laceration
Signs of autonomic activation: diaphoresis, pallor, nausea, warmth
Assess for trauma from fall: head, C-spine, extremities

pairs.value: PE, AAA, arrhythmia, aortic stenosis, seizure, stroke/TIA, hemorrhage
Most common. Vasovagal, situational (cough, micturition, defecation, swallow), carotid sinus hypersensitivity, pain-mediated
Autonomic failure, meds (antihypertensives, diuretics), volume depletion (↓ PO, GI losses, hemorrhage), prolonged standing, hot weather
!!Arrhythmia!! (VT, SVT, AV block, sick sinus, long QT, Brugada, pacer malfunction), !!Structural!! (AS, HOCM, PE, ACS, dissection, tamponade)
Seizure (postictal confusion, lateral tongue bite, incontinence), stroke/TIA, vertebrobasilar insufficiency
Hypoglycemia, hypoxia, intoxication (EtOH, benzo, opioids), hyperventilation, psychiatric, mechanical fall

sections.title: Management
items: If not appearing hypervolemic & low concern for CHF → give IVF bolus
Delegate hx & chart check to teammate while stabilizing
!!Hold antihypertensives & diuretics!! until eval complete and volume status assessed
If dehydrated →!!IVF!! (NS or LR bolus, reassess)
Correct reversible triggers: dehydration, anemia, meds, infection, electrolytes
If arrhythmia identified → treat per ACLS (see Tachycardia / Bradycardia topics)
If EKG shows ischemia → ACS pathway
If concern for PE → anticoagulation, CTPE, consult
If seizure → seizure precautions, neuro consult, consider AEDs
If structural cardiac cause (AS, HOCM) → cardiology consult, avoid vasodilators
If vasovagal & low-risk → reassurance, PO challenge, discharge planning

pairs.value: First & most important test. Look for: non-sinus rhythm, AV block, prolonged QT, Brugada pattern, delta wave, ST changes, RV strain (PE)
Continuous monitoring — arrhythmia may be paroxysmal
Hyponatremia, ↑/↓ K, ↑ Cr (dehydration)
Anemia (hemorrhage), ↑ WBC (infection)
If chest pain, dyspnea, EKG changes, or ischemic concern
Glucose, CBC (anemia), BMP (Na), CK (if seizure suspected)
NT-proBNP (if HF concern), TSH, UA (if older pt), lactate
If concern for PNA, pulm edema, effusions
If head strike, on anticoagulation, focal deficit. Consider brain attack protocol if focal neuro findings
If workup unremarkable & first syncopal event, or if PE concern (dyspnea, tachycardia, RV strain on EKG, asymmetric LE)
r/o AS, HOCM, RV dilation (PE), tamponade. Can be next-day if stable
CTA/CTV or MRA/MRV if vertebrobasilar concern. EEG if seizure suspected. Pacer interrogation if pacer malfunction
Obtain when pt stable enough to stand — supine → standing, wait 3 min

pairs.value: Cardiac syncope (arrhythmia, ACS, PE, structural), hemodynamically unstable, significant injury from fall, anticoag + head strike, recurrent syncope w/o dx
Undifferentiated syncope w/ risk factors, abnormal EKG, first event w/o clear trigger, awaiting workup (echo, trop, tele)
Clear vasovagal or situational trigger, normal EKG, normal vitals, no red flags, no significant injury, tolerating PO, safe home environment
clinical judgment + San Francisco Syncope Rule or Canadian Syncope Risk Score. High risk: abn. EKG, h/o HF, HoTN, Hct <30, SOB, ↑ trop
Cardiology (arrhythmia, structural heart dz, pacer). Neurology (seizure, focal deficits). EP for recurrent unexplained syncope (implantable loop recorder)

topics.title: Abdominal Pain
topics.subtitle: Approach to acute abdominal pain — DDx by location

sections.title: Acute Management
items: !!Airway!! — assess patency; if AMS or active vomiting w/ aspiration risk → position laterally, suction, consider intubation
!!Breathing!! — SpO2, RR; peritonitis or distension can impair diaphragm excursion → ↓ tidal volumes
!!Circulation!! — 2 large-bore IVs;!!assess for shock!! (tachycardia, hypotension, AMS, pallor) → aggressive IVF resuscitation
If peritoneal signs (rigid abdomen, rebound, guarding):!!surgical consult STAT!! — do NOT delay for imaging
If hemodynamically unstable + abdominal pain: think ruptured AAA, ruptured ectopic, massive GI bleed, splenic rupture → emergent imaging/OR
NPO if surgical abdomen suspected
STAT labs: CBC, BMP, lipase, LFTs, lactate, coags, UA, pregnancy test (all women of reproductive age)

sections.title: Clinical Features
items: !!Pregnancy test in ALL females of childbearing age!! — this is mandatory
Vitals: tachycardia and hypotension suggest serious pathology (hemorrhage, sepsis, perforation)
May be associated w/ nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea
Fever may be present in pain from infectious etiology
Elderly and immunocompromised may have minimal sx w/ severe pathology
!!Never withhold analgesia!! — multiple studies confirm it does not mask surgical findings

topics.title: Nausea & Vomiting
topics.subtitle: Approach to acute nausea, vomiting, and inability to tolerate PO

sections.title: Acute Management
items: !!Airway!! — if ↓ LOC or persistent vomiting → position laterally to prevent aspiration; suction PRN
!!Breathing!! — SpO2; assess for aspiration pneumonitis (tachypnea, crackles, hypoxia after vomiting episode)
!!Circulation!! — IV access; assess hydration status (tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, ↓ UOP, orthostatic hypotension)
IV crystalloid bolus (NS or LR) if signs of dehydration or ↓ PO intake
!!Red flags!!: bilious emesis (obstruction), hematemesis (GI bleed), projectile (↑ ICP), severe abdominal pain (surgical abdomen)

sections.title: Red Flags
items: Bilious vomiting in adults = bowel obstruction until proven otherwise
Hematemesis = upper GI bleed — see GI Bleed section
Vomiting + severe headache = SAH, meningitis, ↑ ICP
Vomiting + chest pain = ACS, esophageal rupture (Boerhaave)
Vomiting + abdominal pain = see Abdominal Pain section
Vomiting + altered mentation = metabolic crisis (DKA, Addison's, uremia)

Antiemetic: ondansetron 4 mg IV (!!check QTc first — avoid if QTc >500 ms!!)

topics.title: Fever
topics.subtitle: Approach to fever in the inpatient setting

sections.title: Acute Management
items: !!Airway / Breathing / Circulation!! — fever + hypotension, tachypnea, lactate elevation, or AMS = sepsis until proven otherwise
If meningitis, purpura, septic shock, or toxic appearance → cultures and antibiotics immediately; do not wait for full workup
Fever = temp ≥38.3°C (101°F); critically ill, elderly, immunocompromised, or neutropenic pts may have serious infection without high fever

sections.title: Chart Check / History
items: Timing — new admission fever, post-op day, ICU onset, intermittent vs persistent, relation to transfusion/procedure/drug start
Symptoms by system — cough, dysuria, abdominal pain, diarrhea, headache, rash, wound drainage, rigors
Devices and procedures — central line, Foley, drains, ventilator, dialysis catheter, recent surgery or instrumentation
Immune status — neutropenia, transplant, steroids, chemotherapy, HIV, cirrhosis, splenectomy
Medication review — beta-lactams, anticonvulsants, allopurinol, heparin, antipsychotics, serotonergic agents, anesthetic exposure
Travel, TB exposure, sick contacts, aspiration risk, IV drug use, prosthetic valves/joints
Post-op clues — early fever may be inflammatory; persistent or worsening fever needs source search

pairs.value: Hypotension, tachypnea, mottling, AMS, poor perfusion — sepsis or other critical illness

Remove or evaluate obvious infected devices when clinically appropriate (line, urinary catheter, drain, hardware)

topics.title: Oliguria / Anuria
topics.subtitle: Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr — approach in the inpatient setting

sections.title: First Step — Rule Out Obstruction
items: Airway: assess patency and protect. Breathing: assess WOB, oxygenation, and ventilation. Circulation: assess pulse, BP, perfusion, and IV access.
Bladder scan immediately — if >300-400 mL, place Foley (post-renal is the most reversible cause)
Check Foley if already placed — kinked? clotted? Irrigate or replace
Check fluid status — is the pt hypovolemic? (flat IVC, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes)
Review medications: nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast, ACE/ARB)
Check last creatinine trend — acute vs chronic

pairs.value: Hypovolemia, low cardiac output, hepatorenal syndrome. FENa <1%, BUN/Cr >20. Fluid-responsive.
Ischemia, nephrotoxins. Muddy brown casts. FENa >2%. NOT fluid-responsive.
Bladder outlet obstruction (BPH, stones, malignancy). Bladder scan >400 mL. Foley placement fixes immediately.
Low EF + oliguria — giving fluids may worsen. Check BNP, echo. Inotropes/diuretics depending on profile.

sections.title: Workup
items: Bladder scan — if >300mL, catheterize
BMP (Cr, BUN, K, bicarb), UA w/ microscopy
Urine Na and Cr (for FENa = [urine Na x plasma Cr] / [plasma Na x urine Cr] x 100)
POCUS: IVC for volume status, renal ultrasound for hydronephrosis
Review inputs/outputs — cumulative fluid balance
Hold nephrotoxins, ACE/ARB, NSAIDs

topics.title: Edema
topics.subtitle: Approach to peripheral and pulmonary edema

sections.title: Acute Management
items: !!Airway!! — if anasarca w/ upper airway edema or angioedema → assess for stridor, prepare for advanced airway
!!Breathing!! — SpO2, RR; assess for pulmonary edema, WOB, and need for escalated O2 support
!!Circulation!! — BP, HR; assess for decompensated heart failure (JVD, crackles, S3, hepatojugular reflux)
If hypotensive + edema: consider cardiogenic shock (poor forward flow) → inotropes, NOT more diuretics
Assess for DVT if unilateral leg edema (asymmetric, painful, warm) → Doppler US urgently

pairs.value: Heart failure — BNP, echo, IV diuresis
Cirrhosis / hypoalbuminemia — LFTs, albumin, ultrasound
Nephrotic syndrome — urine protein/Cr, albumin, renal workup
DVT vs cellulitis — duplex ultrasound, D-dimer
Anaphylaxis or ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema — epinephrine, hold ACE
Flash pulmonary edema, ARDS, capillary leak — CXR, BNP, echo

sections.title: Management
items: 1. Sit pt upright and start O2. If flash pulmonary edema is causing marked dyspnea or hypoxemia, start NIPPV early.
2. If BP allows, start nitroglycerin for preload/afterload reduction. Use higher urgency when severe HTN is driving the edema.
3. Give IV loop diuretic if volume overloaded or known CHF: furosemide 40-80 mg IV, or about 2.5× the home PO loop dose.
4. Reassess WOB, BP, urine output, and response. If hypotension, poor perfusion, or worsening respiratory failure develops, think cardiogenic shock and escalate.

drugs.name: Furosemide (Lasix)
drugs.notes: First-line for cardiac edema. Can escalate to drip 5-40 mg/hr if refractory.

drugs.name: Torsemide
drugs.notes: Better oral bioavailability than furosemide. Good for outpt CHF.

drugs.name: Metolazone
drugs.notes: Thiazide-like — synergistic w/ loop diuretics for refractory edema. Watch electrolytes.

drugs.name: Spironolactone
drugs.notes: Potassium-sparing. Adds mortality benefit in systolic CHF (RALES trial). Avoid if K >5.0 or CrCl <30.
